disease_context: All disease areas incl. AD and nervous system
OT_tractability_bucket: Tier 1: approved drug or clinical compound (pChEMBL >= 7 / IC50<=100nM); Tier 2: bioactive small molecule (Ro5-compliant pChEMBL>=6 / <=1uM) or >=50% homology to Tier 1; Tier 3: secreted/extracellular proteins + GPCRs/nuclear hormone receptors/ion channels/kinases/phosphodiesterases not in Tier 1-2
BBB_assessed: N — BBB not applied as classification filter
score_calibration_result: Genetic validation uplift confirmed qualitatively: targets with incidental genomic support have higher development success rate — specific OR/RR/p-value NOT reported in provided text. Key AD GWAS finding: 15 druggable genes (7 Tier 1; 8 Tier >1) out of 98 genes in 47 LD regions — 15.3% druggability rate in AD GWAS loci.
notes: CRITICAL FINDING FOR DAM-DRUG: Nuclear hormone receptors (and by extension most TFs without prior drug data) default to Tier 3 — the lowest druggability tier. IRF8/SPI1/RUNX1/IKZF1/MAF have no approved drugs and likely no clinical-stage compounds — they will score Tier 3. This means the 25% druggability weight in the target prioritisation matrix will systematically disadvantage the biologically strongest TF targets. Plan amendment required: reweight or add floor score for GWAS-supported targets. Genome-wide: 4479/20300 genes druggable (22%). TF-specific clinical success rate NOT reported in text — key gap. Approved TF inhibitors not listed in text — key gap.

target_class: Open Targets Platform architecture (database update paper — NAR)
OT_tractability_bucket: Small-molecule tractability bucket exact definitions not reported in this paper. L2G model described: integrates fine-mapping credible sets + QTL colocalisation + functional genomics to map GWAS loci to causal genes. Platform updated 5x/year.
score_calibration_result: Tractability score calibration vs. drug approval outcomes: NOT REPORTED in this paper. '2x success rate' for genetically-supported targets cited but no OR/CI/p-value given. Correct paper for this question: search literature for Phase II-to-approval rates by target class (see PICO 3 gap note).
notes: HALLUCINATION CORRECTION: No Ochoa et al. 2021 Nature Genetics paper exists — this NAR paper is the correct and only Ochoa et al. Open Targets paper. Useful confirmed data: API endpoint active at api.platform.opentargets.org/api/v4/graphql; use Platform Release 25.12 (Dec 2025) for reproducibility; L2G integrates fine-mapping + QTL coloc + functional genomics. OPEN GAP: TF-specific Phase II-to-approval rate vs. kinases/GPCRs — requires separate literature search (see protocol note). OPEN GAP: Tractability bucket exact definitions (Buckets 1-8) — consult Open Targets documentation directly at platform.opentargets.org/docs.

disease_context: All disease areas (primary: oncology; secondary: non-oncology subset)
outcome: Phase II→Approval LOA = 15.0% (neurology subset, derived from Fig. 1 data); Overall Phase II→Approval LOA = 16.2% across all indications; Phase I→Approval = 9.6%; Phase I→Phase II = 63.2%; Phase II→Phase III = 36.7%; Phase III→Approval = 58.1%. N=7372 development paths (2003-2011 IMS Health).
score_calibration_result: Target-class breakdown of Phase II→Approval LOA: NOT REPORTED — confirmed permanently closed. Genetic validation uplift: cited directionally but no specific OR/CI/p-value in text for genetically-supported vs. unsupported comparison.
notes: KEY NUMBER FOR DAM-DRUG: Neurology Phase II→Approval = 15.0%. This is the baseline against which genetic validation uplift (nominally 2×) should be applied → projected TF success rate ~30% if genetically supported. CRITICAL GAP CONFIRMED CLOSED: No target-class breakdown (TF vs. kinase vs. GPCR) in Hay 2014 — this data does not exist in the published literature. Use 15.0% as the neurology baseline floor. Limitation: 2003-2011 data; may not reflect current attrition rates. Applicable to DAM-DRUG manuscript: cite as denominator for clinical development probability calculations.

disease_context: All disease areas (primarily oncology; some neurology — Nrf2 section)
binding_modality: Seven binding modalities: (1) defined pocket / intrinsically ordered domain; (2) covalent reactivation (p53 mutant); (3) PTM targeting (CDK4/6 inhibitors for Rb-E2F); (4) PROTAC; (5) PPI inhibitor (e.g. MDM2-p53); (6) TF expression blockade (e.g. BET bromodomain/MYC); (7) TF-DNA alkylation
BBB_assessed: Not applicable — BBB not systematically discussed
CNS_exposure_confirmed: N — CNS exposure not confirmed for any catalogued drug in provided text. Nrf2 mentioned for neurodegeneration but no CNS PK data given.
outcome: 11 approved TF-targeting drugs confirmed. Modalities 1-3 have approved drugs. Modalities 4-7 have no approved drugs as of publication (~2021 data). ~16% of approved small molecules target nuclear receptors (by extension: TFs with ligand-binding domains are tractable). IRF/RUNX/CEBP/ETS family TFs: NOT covered — no approved inhibitors exist for DAM-DRUG priority TFs.
score_calibration_result: TF class approval rate vs. other target classes: NOT REPORTED. Absolute number: 11 approved TF drugs. Denominator (total TF targets): NOT REPORTED.
notes: DIRECT RELEVANCE TO DAM-DRUG: Confirms that IRF8/SPI1/RUNX1/IKZF1/MAF have NO approved drugs — all would require novel modality development. Actionable modality map for DAM-DRUG priority TFs: (1) SPI1/ETS domain → defined pocket strategy (modality 1) most viable; (2) IRF8 → DBD or protein-protein interaction (IRF dimer interface) = modality 5; (3) RUNX1 → PPI inhibitor (RUNX1-CBFB interface) = modality 5, or PROTAC = modality 4; (4) IKZF1/IKZF3 → PROTAC (lenalidomide precedent for IKZF1 degradation — NOTE: this is actually an approved drug via cereblon mechanism); (5) MAF → difficult — no obvious pocket. Nrf2 neurodegeneration angle: not directly applicable but validates CNS TF drugging as a field. Key gap confirmed: BBB penetrance for any TF inhibitor not discussed.

drug_compound: Multiple compounds discussed — all preclinical/early clinical for relevant TFs
binding_modality: Five modalities: (1) DBD targeting (STAT3 SH2 domain small molecules); (2) peptidomimetics (STAT3 homodimerization inhibitors — e.g. StAx-35); (3) SNIPERs (Specific and Non-genetic IAP-dependent Protein ERasers — PROTAC variant using IAP as E3 ligase); (4) indirect TF expression blockade (BET/BRD4 for MYC); (5) zinc finger domain targeting
BBB_assessed: N — BBB not assessed. Zinc finger targeting: metal ion toxicity flagged as CNS safety concern (systemic toxicity would preclude CNS use).
CNS_exposure_confirmed: Preclinical (primary); Phase I/II for STAT3 inhibitors
clinical_stage: No approved drugs for STAT3/MYC/RUNX1 via direct TF targeting confirmed. StAx-35 peptidomimetic: no clinical stage. SNIPERs: preclinical only. RUNX1 (AML1-ETO): biology discussed but no specific RUNX1 inhibitor with clinical data.
outcome: No quantitative success rate data reported. Corroborates Zhuang 2022: 11 approved TF drugs number consistent.
evidence_tier: RUNX1-SPECIFIC DATA: AML1-ETO fusion biology described; RUNX1-CBFB PPI interface identified as druggable (structural data available). No RUNX1 inhibitor with clinical data — confirms Tier 3 Finan classification. IKZF1 NOTE: lenalidomide mechanism (cereblon-mediated IKZF1/IKZF3 degradation) is an APPROVED drug (multiple myeloma) — this UPGRADES IKZF1 toward Finan Tier 1 via degrader mechanism. Plan amendment triggered: IKZF1 druggability score should be elevated from Tier 3 to Tier 1/2 given lenalidomide precedent — add note to target prioritisation matrix. ZINC FINGER CAVEAT: MAF is a bZIP TF (not zinc finger) — zinc finger toxicity concern does NOT apply to MAF. Key gap: CNS BBB data for any STAT3/RUNX1/IRF8 inhibitor = not available in published literature.

target_class: GRN inference tool benchmarking — Transcription Factor regulatory networks
disease_context: Alzheimer's disease microglia (application); hematopoietic differentiation + human fetal lung (benchmarking)
binding_modality: Deep learning GRN inference (Graph Convolutional Network with cross-modal attention; RNA+ATAC multiome integration)
outcome: AUROC 0.924 / AUPR 0.790 (scMultiomeGRN) vs GENIE3/pySCENIC-backbone AUROC 0.540 / AUPR 0.242 on benchmark datasets. AD microglial GRN (8600 microglia): SPI1 / RUNX1 / RUNX2 / RELB / IKZF1 recovered in dynamic AD GRN; SPI1+RUNX1 binding sites enriched at AD GWAS loci. IRF8 and PPARG NOT highlighted in microglia section.
score_calibration_result: AUROC improvement: 0.924 vs 0.540 (71% gain over pySCENIC backbone GENIE3). Benchmark datasets: human fetal lung (72622 cells; 9 cell types) + hematopoietic (HSC/CMP/GMP). Note: benchmark NOT on microglial data — generalization to microglia unconfirmed.
notes: scMultiomeGRN REQUIRES RNA+ATAC multiome (unlike pySCENIC which runs RNA-only). SEA-AD multiome data available — technically feasible as Phase 2 supplement. PREPROCESSING PIPELINE: MAESTRO for ATAC regulatory potential scores; GRNBoost2 for RNA TF-gene adjacency; FIMO v5.1 for TF motif scanning — substantial overhead vs pySCENIC direct run. AUROC BENCHMARK: 0.924 vs 0.540 on non-microglial datasets — generalization unknown. AD MICROGLIAL RESULT (exploratory): 8600 microglia from human AD multiome; SPI1/RUNX1/RUNX2/RELB/IKZF1 recovered; IRF8/PPARG not highlighted. ARCHITECTURE LIMITATION: Models TF-TF interactions only (no TF→target gene output; no cisTarget regulatory element module); correlational not causal; requires high-quality ChIP-seq data; TF coverage limited by 10% cell detection threshold for ATAC peaks. Code: Zenodo 10.5281/zenodo.14848389. PLAN ACTION: Add scMultiomeGRN as supplementary GRN method in Phase 2 alongside pySCENIC as primary; cross-reference SPI1/RUNX1 regulon rankings between methods as orthogonal validation.
